business_info: For gallery curation purposes, an artist’s “impact score” is sometimes approximated by counting how many different set codes they appear in, but this is only used informally by the creative team and is not stored as a numeric metric in the database.

In creative retrospectives, “signature pieces” are usually defined as the first card an artist illustrates in a new block that later receives a promo reprint, though some art directors stretch this to include any early card that becomes iconic in Commander.

Internal dashboards sometimes rank illustrators by the total number of distinct card UUIDs they are associated with, ignoring alternate art and promo printings that share the same base illustration id.

When evaluating how well an artist handles “large scale” subjects, creative leads often look at cards where the rules text references power 6 or greater, even if the creature is not visually huge in the illustration itself.

Marketing sometimes refers to certain iconic creature lines (like dragons, angels, hydras, titans) as “tentpole threats” in presentations, but this label is only used in slide decks and not encoded anywhere in the schema.

Creative briefs occasionally distinguish between “battlefield monsters” (huge, central threats) and “set dressing creatures” (background fauna), but there is no reliable text field or flag in the database that captures this distinction.
The art team uses the phrase “monstrous silhouette” for illustrations where the creature occupies more than 60% of the frame height, regardless of the creature type printed on the card.

For historical reports on fantasy tropes, any card whose English name contains the substring "Dragon" is grouped with other draconic entities such as drakes and wurms, even if their in-game creature type is not literally Dragon.

From a creative-ops perspective, “artist tenure” is sometimes approximated by the earliest originalReleaseDate of any card they illustrated, but this is complicated by backdated promos and reprints that reuse older art.

Concept push documents may tag certain commissions as “scale studies” when the brief is focused on communicating size relative to the environment, but this annotation lives only in internal spreadsheets and never makes it into the card data.

In some moodboards, giant creatures that dominate the frame are loosely grouped under the label “mega threats,” but this is an art direction term only and not something analysts can filter for using the database.
User research decks sometimes describe “spectacle cards” as those whose art depicts a single central figure with explosive motion or scale, but there is no database flag to indicate spectacle status.

For anniversary showcases, curators occasionally approximate an artist’s “big creature rate” by manually tagging a sample of their cards where the creature’s head rises above the frame’s text box, but those tags never get loaded back into production systems.

In internal writeups, the phrase “plane-defining monster” usually refers to a creature that appears on multiple key-art pieces or marketing banners for a plane, regardless of how many card printings it actually has.

Some art leads keep personal spreadsheets where they categorize each of their favorite illustrators into rough archetypes like “dragon specialist,” “horror specialist,” or “environmentalist,” but these labels are subjective and never imported into the database.

For internal highlight reels, curators sometimes define an illustrator’s “showcase density” as the proportion of their cards that have appeared in any marketing splash image, but this metric is tracked only in presentation notes.

Where analysts need a rough heuristic for “prolific artists,” they sometimes count distinct combinations of artist + setCode in the cards table, which treats multiple printings within a set as a single contribution.

The creative team occasionally references an informal “dragon density” metric for a set, computed as the percentage of its cards whose English name contains the word "Dragon" or whose primary key art depicts a dragon-like creature.

In some legacy reports, cards whose names contain terms like “Colossus,” “Titan,” or “Behemoth” were bulk-tagged as “macro threats,” but those tags were never standardized and do not appear in the current schema.

Brand documents sometimes treat “mythic monsters” as creatures that combined high rarity with marquee art, but there is no single database field that can be used to reproduce the exact historical lists they used.

Artist showcase decks for conventions may cluster cards by “scale impression” (small, medium, huge) based on curator judgment; these labels are hand-written in slide notes rather than pulled from structured data.

When building “battle of the giants” theme pages, web editors often filter first by creature type (Giant, Leviathan, Eldrazi, etc.) and then manually add any card whose illustration clearly reads as enormous, regardless of its printed type line.

Some older creative briefs flagged commissions as “kaiju-scale” if the monster was supposed to dwarf cityscapes or fortresses, but this tag lived only in the art request text and was not systematically captured elsewhere.

There have been experiments with tagging art as “skyscape,” “ground battle,” or “close-up portrait,” but those trial tags were applied inconsistently across sets and never made it into the production pipeline.

For internal concept art tracking, some teams used to maintain a separate table of “monster studies” with links back to card uuids, but these records were archived to static documents and are no longer joined in analytics queries.
In lore-driven event kits, “world-ending threats” are curated manually by the narrative team from story spotlights, without relying on any one rules text phrase or card type to define the category.
Digital product pages for “monster bundles” often include any card where the primary art clearly shows claws, fangs, or tentacles, regardless of whether the name explicitly references a creature.

Some experimental dashboards tried to rate “perceived size” of creatures based on how far the camera appears to be pulled back in the illustration, but the scoring rubric varied so much by set that those numbers were never shipped.

In some curated art books, editors will treat multiple reprints of the same illustration as a single “piece,” even if the underlying database stores them as separate card records for different sets or promos.

Occasionally, the same large creature concept is depicted across several cards in a cycle (e.g., different phases of a titan), but from a data perspective these remain distinct card entries linked only by shared naming patterns and flavor text.

For high-level portfolio reviews, art directors sometimes talk about an illustrator’s “monster to non-monster ratio,” but this is based on quick visual scans of their card gallery, not on a formal database definition of what counts as a monster.
Some early attempts at tagging “crowd scenes” in art looked for cards whose rules text made multiple creature tokens, but reviewers found too many false positives and the effort was abandoned.
